network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as a highly convergent prebiotic target produced through ten literature-grounded routes centered on shared hubs: hydrogen cyanide, formaldehyde, ammonia, aminoacetonitrile, and glyoxylate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks (formate, pyruvate, glyoxylate) through greigite/magnetite/awaruite-like mineral catalysis under serpentinizing conditions, while surface environments generate and concentrate HCN-driven chemistry via atmospheric discharge, eutectic freezing, cyanosulfidic photochemistry, and Strecker/Bucherer-Bergs processes on mineral surfaces. Biochemical/prebiotic settings complete slower hydrolyses and enable peptide assembly from glycine. The network avoids unsupported bridges such as pyruvate-to-glyoxylate oxidation under anoxic vents and instead uses literature-backed glyoxylate formation/availability and ferroan brucite-mediated glycine production.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline serpentinizing conditions, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 gives formate as a primary C1 fixation product.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 fixation to formate under alkaline hydrothermal conditions at about 100C. This is a core literature-supported hydrothermal entry into glycine precursor space.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal carbon fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; pyruvate observed at low micromolar yield

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-catalyzed CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation yield a C3 alpha-keto acid pool including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2/H2 on hydrothermal minerals, and Beyazay et al. (2023) further supported room-temperature CO2 fixation to pyruvate on Ni3Fe particles under pressure. Pyruvate is included as a hydrothermal metabolic hub, but not forced into unsupported oxidation to glyoxylate.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon pool to formaldehyde

conditions.other: speculative network-connecting reduction/oxidation equilibration within vent-derived C1 pool

reactions.mechanism: Vent-generated reduced C1 species are represented as feeding a formaldehyde pool transferable to surface settings.
reactions.reasoning: The literature strongly supports hydrothermal generation of reduced C1 compounds such as formate and methanol (Preiner et al., 2020), but selective formaldehyde accumulation is less directly demonstrated. This reaction is therefore explicitly speculative and included only as a minimal connector between hydrothermal C1 fixation and the formaldehyde-centered Strecker network described by Miller (1953, 1957) and Magrino et al. (2021).

reactions.name: Atmospheric discharge and mineral trapping produce HCN and ammonia feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and montmorillonite as photochemical/adsorptive mineral surfaces
conditions.other: spark/UV-active atmosphere with condensation into ponds; weakly reducing or CO-containing regimes

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric chemistry generates dissolved HCN and NH3, which are retained and concentrated on mineral-bearing surface waters.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955, 1957), Johnson et al. (2008), Parker et al. (2011), and Schlesinger & Miller (1983) support atmospheric generation of HCN and NH3 precursors under spark-discharge conditions, especially in reducing or CO-containing mixtures. Mineral agents here function as surface concentrators/photochemical partners in compliance with the surface-environment requirement rather than as the primary energetic source.

reactions.name: Formaldehyde and ammonia condensation to aminomethanol

conditions.other: warm pond or evaporitic pool, pH near neutral to mildly basic

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to formaldehyde forms carbinolamine aminomethanol.
reactions.reasoning: The aminomethanol intermediate is part of the canonical Strecker mechanism established from Miller's chemistry and explicitly mapped by Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021). Mineral surfaces are included as plausible concentrators in surface ponds.

reactions.name: Aminomethanol dehydration to methanimine

conditions.other: mild drying or fluctuating hydration supports imine formation

reactions.mechanism: Carbinolamine dehydration produces methanimine, the electrophile for cyanide addition.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) explicitly describe aminomethanol dehydration to methanimine in aqueous Strecker synthesis. Surface minerals plausibly aid concentration and water removal during wet-dry fluctuations.

reactions.name: Cyanide addition to methanimine gives aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions; glycinonitrile formation barrier about 15 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to methanimine forms aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the central Strecker step for glycine, supported by Miller (1953, 1957) and quantitatively characterized by Magrino et al. (2021), who identified glycinonitrile formation as kinetically accessible with an approximately 15 kcal/mol barrier.

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile hydration to glycinamide

conditions.other: slow aqueous hydrolysis; nitrile hydration barrier about 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydration converts aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) mapped the hydrolysis sequence from aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide in explicit water and identified this as a major kinetic stage on the path to glycine.

reactions.name: Glycinamide hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: final hydrolysis is rate-limiting with barrier about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis yields free glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino et al. (2021) identified the glycinamide-to-glycine step as the highest-barrier stage of Strecker hydrolysis, about 31 kcal/mol, but overall strongly exergonic.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs carboxylative conversion of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: surface bicarbonate/carbonate milieu with mineral concentration
conditions.other: bicarbonate-rich water, pH about 8

reactions.mechanism: CO2 traps aminoacetonitrile in a cyclic ureide reservoir as hydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) showed that under prebiotic HCN/HCHO/NH3 conditions, CO2 promotes productive hydantoin formation from the small aminoacetonitrile fraction; Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated efficient hydantoin formation in bicarbonate buffer, validating the Bucherer-Bergs branch as a realistic route.

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: extended hydrolysis in pooled aqueous settings

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of hydantoin forms N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs route proceeds through hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine before releasing glycine, as summarized by Taillades et al. (1998) and connected to modern prebiotic hydantoin chemistry by Pulletikurti et al. (2022).

reactions.name: N-carbamoylglycine hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: slow de-carbamoylation/hydrolysis in aqueous pools

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic deprotection of N-carbamoylglycine yields glycine.
reactions.reasoning: This terminal Bucherer-Bergs step is part of the literature-described hydantoin route to glycine (Taillades et al., 1998).

reactions.name: Eutectic concentration of HCN and ammonia to ammonium cyanide

conditions.other: freezing to eutectic conditions near -23.4C strongly concentrates HCN

reactions.mechanism: Freezing concentrates cyanide and ammonia into reactive eutectic brines represented here as an ammonium cyanide reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) established the concentration threshold for HCN polymerization and emphasized eutectic enrichment; Levy et al. (2000) later showed frozen NH4CN solutions yield glycine over long timescales. Mineral agents serve as brine-interface concentrators in surface ice settings.

reactions.name: HCN polymerization in concentrated/eutectic media

conditions.other: productive above about 0.01-0.1 M HCN; can occur in eutectic brines or warm basic solutions

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalyzed HCN oligomerization generates a heterogeneous polymer/oligomer reservoir rich in amino acid precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) quantified HCN oligomerization kinetics and concentration thresholds, while Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) showed hydrolysis of HCN polymers gives glycine as the dominant amino acid product under diverse conditions. A fixed molecular formula is intentionally avoided because the polymer is heterogeneous.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN polymer reservoir to glycine

conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis of polymeric HCN products

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic cleavage of HCN polymer backbones releases glycine among amino acid products.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine is the most abundant amino acid obtained from hydrolyzed HCN polymers across 23 conditions; this builds on Oró's early HCN polymer chemistry and Matthews & Moser (1966).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer pool hydrolysis to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: partial hydrolysis/rearrangement of oligomer reservoir

reactions.mechanism: A subset of HCN oligomers serves as a reservoir feeding back into aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Belloche et al. (2008) detected aminoacetonitrile in interstellar chemistry and the glycine literature identifies it as a stable reservoir precursor. Direct release from specific HCN oligomers is less resolved experimentally than bulk glycine release, so this connector is moderately speculative but consistent with the reported reservoir role of aminoacetonitrile in HCN-based networks.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals and phosphate-buffered aqueous medium
conditions.other: UV irradiation; cyanosulfidic network chemistry

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reductive homologation in HCN/H2S/phosphate systems generates aminonitrile precursors including aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated unified cyanosulfidic chemistry producing amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid precursors, including aminoacetonitrile as the glycine precursor, under HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate conditions at about 35C.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of formamide to HCN-ammonia precursor pool

conditions.other: UV-active shallow pool chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Formamide acts as a condensed C-N reservoir feeding cyanide/ammonia chemistry under surface photochemical processing.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies formamide as an HCN/H2O/UV product and a versatile prebiotic feedstock (Bernstein et al., 2002; Senanayake & Idriss, 2006). This reaction is a conservative connector back into the HCN/NH3 hub rather than an unsupported direct formamide-to-glycine step.

reactions.name: UV hydration of HCN to formamide

conditions.other: UV irradiation in shallow surface waters

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical hydration/conversion of HCN in water generates formamide as a durable C-N intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research summary highlights UV photochemistry of HCN + H2O to formamide and subsequent broader prebiotic chemistry; TiO2 photochemistry is supported by Senanayake & Idriss (2006) for formamide-derived organics and by Bernstein et al. (2002) for UV-driven ice chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal-serpentinite availability of glyoxylate

conditions.other: represents glyoxylate availability in serpentinizing systems; pathway details remain an open area

reactions.mechanism: Reduced carbon fixation in serpentinite-associated systems supplies alpha-keto/oxoacid pools including glyoxylate available for subsequent amination.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature strongly supports hydrothermal generation of reduced carbon pools in serpentinizing systems (Preiner et al., 2020; Beyazay et al., 2023) and specifically identifies glyoxylate as the substrate in the 2024 ferroan brucite glycine synthesis study. Because a single definitive vent route to glyoxylate is not specified in the report, this node represents literature-necessary availability rather than a fully resolved selective step.

reactions.name: Ferroan brucite-mediated synthesis of glycine from glyoxylate and nitrate

conditions.other: pH about 8-9; glycine largely partitions to mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination/reduction of nitrate and glyoxylate yields glycine while adsorbing product to the mineral.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) showed ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate plus glyoxylate with up to 97% of glycine partitioning to the mineral fraction. This is one of the strongest direct hydrothermal glycine routes in the supplied literature.

reactions.name: UV-driven reversible amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.catalyst: UV-active mineral surfaces; ZnS-like photochemical analog represented within sulfide mineral class
conditions.other: UV illumination in shallow pools

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical amination of the alpha-oxo acid glyoxylate yields glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) demonstrated UV-driven reversible amination of alpha-keto acids, specifically glyoxylate to glycine, on ZnS. Because the allowed surface mineral classes require sulfide minerals and TiO2, the reaction is represented with sulfide mineral photochemistry plus TiO2 as a compatible UV-active surface; the mechanistic basis remains the literature-supported photochemical amination of glyoxylate.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of HCN and formaldehyde precursors on metallic minerals

conditions.other: FTT chemistry in hot zones followed by transport to cooler zones for Strecker-type reactions

reactions.mechanism: High-temperature catalytic gas-surface chemistry generates simple carbonyl and nitrile precursors that are transferred to cooler aqueous environments.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) described FTT-type amino acid synthesis on meteoritic iron catalysts, and the supplied report specifically frames the mechanism as FTT generation of formaldehyde and HCN on catalyst surfaces followed by Strecker synthesis in cooler zones. This reaction is therefore kept at the precursor-pool level rather than an overcompressed direct glycine synthesis.

reactions.name: Long-term frozen ammonium cyanide chemistry yielding glycine

conditions.other: decadal frozen storage in eutectic ice matrices

reactions.mechanism: Slow frozen-state chemistry and hydrolysis of ammonium cyanide reservoirs produce glycine directly.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) detected glycine from NH4CN solutions frozen for 25 years at -20C and -78C, providing a distinct ice-mediated pathway separate from warm-solution Strecker processing.

reactions.name: Interstellar-ice analog photochemistry to aminoacetonitrile precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: mineral capture of photochemical products after ice processing
conditions.other: UV photolysis of ice analog compositions followed by warming and aqueous transfer

reactions.mechanism: Photochemically processed H2O/NH3/HCN/carbonyl ices generate aminonitrile reservoirs, represented here by aminoacetonitrile entering surface aqueous chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Bernstein et al. (2002) and Elsila et al. (2007) showed UV photolysis of interstellar ice analogs produces glycine and related precursors through multiple mechanisms. Belloche et al. (2008) detected aminoacetonitrile in interstellar material. This route is included as a literature-supported precursor-delivery branch into the terrestrial aminoacetonitrile hub.

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide-forming condensation of glycine

conditions.pressure: 1 atm to 10 MPa depending on setting
conditions.other: wet-dry cycling or short hydrothermal residence; dimerization optimum around pH 9.8 and 140C

reactions.mechanism: Glycine condenses to peptide-like products under dehydration or short high-temperature pulses.
reactions.reasoning: Sakata et al. (2010) determined favorable glycine dimerization conditions around pH 9.8 and 140C, while Imai et al. (1999) generated oligoglycines at 270C and 10 MPa in hydrothermal flow. This reaction links glycine synthesis to downstream biochemical assembly.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker pathway
pathways.description: The dominant glycine pathway: formaldehyde and ammonia form aminomethanol, dehydrate to methanimine, cyanide addition yields aminoacetonitrile, and aqueous hydrolysis gives glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs branch from aminoacetonitrile
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2 as hydantoin, then hydrolyzed through N-carbamoylglycine to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated ammonium cyanide polymerizes in eutectic or concentrated surface settings, and hydrolysis of the polymer reservoir releases glycine directly.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer to aminoacetonitrile route
pathways.description: HCN oligomers formed in concentrated cyanide chemistry feed back into the aminoacetonitrile hub, which then hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox route
pathways.description: Unified HCN-H2S-UV-phosphate chemistry forms aminoacetonitrile directly, then aqueous hydrolysis yields glycine.

pathways.name: Ferroan brucite glyoxylate-nitrate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal serpentinite systems supply glyoxylate, and ferroan brucite mediates direct glycine synthesis from glyoxylate plus nitrate.

pathways.name: Photochemical glyoxylate amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glyoxylate transferred to surface UV settings is aminated photochemically to glycine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: High-temperature catalytic CO/H2/NH3 chemistry generates HCN and formaldehyde precursors, which enter the standard Strecker sequence to glycine.

pathways.name: Frozen ammonium cyanide direct route
pathways.description: Ammonium cyanide concentrated in eutectic ice slowly converts to glycine over long frozen intervals.

pathways.name: Interstellar-ice precursor delivery to Strecker hydrolysis
pathways.description: UV-processed ice analog chemistry provides aminoacetonitrile to terrestrial aqueous settings, where hydrolysis yields glycine.
